title: Rapid Development -- Ship Fast, Iterate Fast
description: Stop overthinking, start shipping. Templates, tools, and tactics to go from zero to deployed in minutes, not months.
keyPoints: Ship an MVP before perfecting anything
Use starter templates and scaffolding to skip boilerplate
Leverage AI to generate code, tests, and debug faster
Deploy in seconds with modern CLI tools
Know when speed creates debt and when it creates momentum

blocks.heading: The Speed Mindset
blocks.body: The fastest developers are not the ones who type the fastest. They are the ones who build the smallest possible thing that works, ship it, get feedback, and iterate. Every hour you spend perfecting a feature nobody has used yet is an hour wasted. Your first version will be wrong. Accept that. Ship it anyway.

blocks.title: The Golden Rule of Rapid Development
blocks.body: If you are not embarrassed by the first version of your product, you launched too late. Get something real in front of real users as fast as humanly possible. Their feedback is worth more than a month of planning.

blocks.heading: MVP vs Perfect -- When to Cut Corners
rows: Pixel-perfect design -- use a component library
Authentication and authorization -- never roll your own crypto
Full test coverage -- test critical paths only
Input validation -- never trust user input
Microservices architecture -- start with a monolith
HTTPS and secure headers -- security from day one
Custom design system -- use Tailwind and shadcn/ui
Database backups -- you will lose data eventually
Internationalization -- launch in one language first
Advanced caching -- optimize when you have traffic
Error boundaries -- catch crashes before users see them

blocks.body: Perfect is the enemy of shipped. A working prototype in a users hands teaches you more than six months of architecture planning. The goal is not to build the final product on day one. The goal is to learn what the final product should be.

blocks.heading: Starter Templates -- Full Project in 60 Seconds
blocks.body: Stop starting from scratch. Every major framework has a CLI that scaffolds a production-ready project with routing, TypeScript, linting, and styling already configured. Pick one and run it.

blocks.description: Creates a full Next.js app with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, ESLint, App Router, and src directory. You are coding in under 60 seconds.
blocks.code:
npx create-next-app@latest my-app --typescript --tailwind --eslint --app --src-dir --use-npm
cd my-app
npm run dev

blocks.description: Vite is blazing fast. Hot module replacement in milliseconds, not seconds. Great for SPAs where you do not need server-side rendering.
blocks.code:
npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts
cd my-app
npm install
npm run dev

blocks.description: The T3 Stack gives you Next.js, TypeScript, tRPC, Prisma, Tailwind, and NextAuth all wired together. Full-stack type safety out of the box.
blocks.code:
npm create t3-app@latest my-app
cd my-app
npm run dev

blocks.description: After creating a Next.js app, run shadcn init to set up a beautiful component system. Then add components one at a time as you need them.
blocks.code:
npx shadcn@latest init
npx shadcn@latest add button card input dialog table
# Components are copied into your project -- you own and customize them

blocks.title: Pro Tip -- Pick a Stack and Stick With It
blocks.body: Do not spend three days comparing frameworks. Pick Next.js + Tailwind + shadcn/ui if you have no strong preference. It is the most popular combo, has the most tutorials, and the most StackOverflow answers. You can always switch later (but you probably will not need to).

blocks.heading: Copy-Paste Development -- No Shame, All Speed
blocks.body: The fastest code is code you did not write. Component libraries like shadcn/ui give you production-quality UI components that you copy directly into your project. No npm dependency, no version conflicts, full customization. Just paste and go.

blocks.description: A complete dashboard page built entirely from shadcn/ui components. You wrote zero CSS. Zero custom components. It looks professional and it took five minutes.
blocks.code:
import { Card, CardContent, CardHeader, CardTitle } from '@/components/ui/card'; import { Button } from '@/components/ui/button'; import { Badge } from '@/components/ui/badge'; export default function DashboardPage() { return (

Dashboard

Revenue

$12,450

+12% this month Users

1,234

+8% this week Active Now

42

Live
); }

cards.title: shadcn/ui
cards.body: Copy-paste React components built on Radix UI and Tailwind. You own every line. No dependency lock-in. The gold standard for rapid UI development.

cards.title: Tailwind UI
cards.body: Premium templates and components from the Tailwind team. Hundreds of responsive, accessible blocks you drop into your project. Worth every dollar for speed.

cards.title: MUI Templates
cards.body: Material UI offers free and premium templates for dashboards, landing pages, and e-commerce. Battle-tested components with theming built in.

cards.title: v0 by Vercel
cards.body: Describe a UI in plain English and get a working React component with shadcn/ui and Tailwind. AI-generated copy-paste code that actually looks good.

cards.title: Aceternity UI
cards.body: Animated, modern UI components you copy-paste into your project. Scroll effects, hero sections, cards with glowing borders -- the fancy stuff made easy.

cards.title: Magic UI
cards.body: Beautiful animated components built on top of shadcn/ui and Tailwind. Text animations, number tickers, gradient borders -- all copy-paste ready.

blocks.heading: AI-Assisted Speed -- Your Fastest Teammate
blocks.body: AI does not replace you. It replaces the boring parts. Boilerplate, tests, documentation, regex, SQL queries, CSS layouts -- all the stuff that slows you down and does not require creative thinking. Use AI for the grunt work so you can focus on the product.

blocks.heading: What AI Does Well (Use It Here)
items: Generate boilerplate -- CRUD endpoints, form components, API routes, database schemas
Write tests -- Give it your function, ask for unit tests covering edge cases
Debug errors -- Paste a stack trace and get an explanation plus fix in seconds
Scaffold entire features -- Describe what you want in plain English, get working code
Convert between formats -- JSON to TypeScript types, SQL to Prisma schema, REST to tRPC
Write documentation -- Give it your code, get JSDoc comments and README sections
Regex and complex logic -- Describe the pattern, get the expression with explanation

blocks.description: Copy these prompts directly into Claude or ChatGPT. Be specific about your stack, include example data, and ask for TypeScript. The more context you give, the better the output.
blocks.code:
PROMPT 1 -- Scaffold an API:
"Create a Next.js API route at /api/posts that handles GET (list all, with
pagination), POST (create), and DELETE (by id). Use Prisma with a Post model
that has id, title, content, published, and createdAt. Include error handling
and return proper HTTP status codes. TypeScript."

PROMPT 2 -- Generate tests:
"Write Jest tests for this function. Cover: valid input, empty input, null,
undefined, very large numbers, negative numbers, and string input. Use
describe/it blocks with clear test names.
[paste your function here]"

PROMPT 3 -- Debug an error:
"I am getting this error in my Next.js app. Explain what is happening and
give me the fix:
[paste the full error message and stack trace]"

PROMPT 4 -- Convert formats:
"Convert this SQL schema to a Prisma schema. Preserve all relationships,
defaults, and constraints:
[paste SQL]"

blocks.title: AI Code Needs Review
blocks.body: AI-generated code is a first draft, not a final product. Always review it for security issues (SQL injection, XSS), hardcoded values, missing error handling, and incorrect business logic. Trust but verify. The speed gain comes from editing AI output, not blindly shipping it.

blocks.heading: The Rapid Prototyping Stack
blocks.body: You need a stack where every layer talks to every other layer with full type safety, zero configuration, and instant feedback. Two combos dominate rapid prototyping right now.

The type-safe full-stack combo. Change your database schema, and your frontend code gets type errors instantly if it is using the wrong fields. No REST endpoints to maintain, no OpenAPI specs, no code generation. Just end-to-end TypeScript.

blocks.description: A complete tRPC router with full CRUD. Your frontend gets autocomplete on every field name, every input, every return type. Change the schema and TypeScript catches every broken call site.

blocks.body: When you do not want to write any backend code at all. Supabase gives you a Postgres database, authentication, file storage, and real-time subscriptions. You call it directly from your frontend with a typed client.

blocks.description: Set up the Supabase client once, use it everywhere. The Database type is auto-generated from your schema so you get full autocomplete.

blocks.heading: Backend in 5 Minutes -- Zero Server Code
blocks.body: You do not need to build a backend. Not for your MVP, not for your prototype, maybe not ever. Backend-as-a-Service platforms give you a database, auth, file storage, and APIs with zero server code. Pick one, connect it, and start building your UI.

cards.title: Supabase
cards.body: Open-source Firebase alternative. Postgres database, Row Level Security, Auth, Storage, Edge Functions, and real-time subscriptions. Generous free tier. The go-to choice for most projects.

cards.title: Firebase
cards.body: Google's BaaS. Firestore (NoSQL), Authentication, Cloud Functions, Hosting, and Analytics. Massive ecosystem. Best if you are already in the Google Cloud world or building mobile apps.

cards.title: PocketBase
cards.body: A single binary that gives you a database, auth, file storage, and admin dashboard. Download it, run it, done. Perfect for side projects and self-hosted apps.

cards.title: Convex
cards.body: Reactive backend with real-time sync, scheduled functions, and file storage. TypeScript-native. Your frontend automatically updates when data changes. Zero polling, zero WebSocket setup.

blocks.description: Supabase from zero to running in four commands. After this, you have a full Postgres database with a REST API, a GraphQL API, auth endpoints, and a dashboard to manage it all.
blocks.code:
npm install @supabase/supabase-js
npx supabase init
npx supabase start
# Local Supabase is running -- dashboard at http://localhost:54323
# Create tables in the dashboard, get instant API endpoints

blocks.heading: Authentication in 2 Minutes
blocks.body: Auth is the feature developers waste the most time building from scratch. Do not. Use a library that handles sessions, tokens, OAuth providers, password hashing, email verification, and rate limiting. Your job is to call a function, not to understand PKCE flows.

blocks.description: Full GitHub + Google login in one file. NextAuth handles callbacks, sessions, CSRF protection, and token rotation. You add providers and you are done.
A sign-in button. That is literally all the frontend auth code you need.
Clerk gives you a complete auth UI out of the box. Sign-in modals, user profile pages, organization management -- all pre-built. Wrap your app and drop in components.
If you are already using Supabase for your database, auth is built in. Social login, magic links, and email/password with zero extra setup.

blocks.heading: Database Without SQL -- Prisma in 3 Commands
blocks.body: Prisma lets you define your database schema in a simple file, generate a migration, and immediately query your data with full TypeScript autocompletion. No SQL, no ORMs with mysterious methods, no raw query strings. Schema to type-safe queries in three commands.

blocks.title: Define Your Schema
blocks.body: Write your data model in plain English-like syntax. Prisma handles the SQL under the hood.

blocks.title: Run Migration
blocks.body: One command creates the tables and generates the typed client.
blocks.code:
npx prisma migrate dev --name init
# Creates tables in your database
# Generates PrismaClient with full TypeScript types

blocks.title: Query With Full Type Safety
blocks.body: Every query is autocompleted. Every result is typed. If you mistype a field name, TypeScript catches it before you even run the code.

blocks.heading: Deploy in 30 Seconds
blocks.body: Your app is not real until it is on the internet. Local demos do not count. Deploy early, deploy often. Modern platforms make this absurdly easy -- one command, one click, or just push to git.

blocks.description: One command. Vercel auto-detects Next.js, builds it, deploys it, and gives you a URL. Every git push to main auto-deploys. Every pull request gets a preview URL.
blocks.code:
npm i -g vercel
vercel
# Answer the prompts (or just hit Enter for defaults)
# Deployed. You have a live URL.

blocks.description: Netlify works great for static sites and Jamstack apps. Drag and drop your build folder or use the CLI.
blocks.code:
npm i -g netlify-cli
ntl init
ntl deploy --prod
# Or just drag your 'out' folder to app.netlify.com/drop

blocks.description: Railway is great for full-stack apps that need a database. It provisions Postgres, Redis, or any other service alongside your app.
blocks.code:
npm i -g @railway/cli
railway login
railway init
railway up
# Deployed with a database. Environment variables are auto-injected.

blocks.description: Fly.io deploys Docker containers to servers worldwide. Great for apps that need to be close to users globally.
blocks.code:
curl -L https://fly.io/install.sh | sh
fly launch
fly deploy
# Running on servers worldwide

blocks.title: Preview Deployments Are a Superpower
blocks.body: Vercel and Netlify create a unique URL for every pull request. Share it with your team, your client, your mom. They can see exactly what changed without pulling code or running anything locally. This is the fastest feedback loop in web development.

blocks.heading: Pre-Built Features -- Payments, Emails, Uploads, Real-Time
blocks.body: Do not build what you can buy or import. Payments, email sending, file uploads, and real-time features are solved problems. Plug in a service, call an API, and move on to the features that make your app unique.

blocks.description: A complete payment flow. Stripe handles the checkout page, card validation, 3D Secure, receipts, and fraud detection. You redirect the user and handle the webhook.
Resend is email for developers. No SMTP config, no templates to manage. Send beautiful emails with React components or plain HTML.

blocks.title: UploadThing -- File Uploads Without the Pain

blocks.description: File uploads are notoriously annoying. UploadThing handles presigned URLs, validation, size limits, and type checking. You define what is allowed, it handles the rest.
Add real-time updates to your app without WebSocket boilerplate. Pusher handles connections, reconnections, scaling, and fallbacks.

blocks.heading: Scaffolding Tools -- Generate, Do Not Type
blocks.body: If you are creating the same file structure over and over -- a new component with its styles, tests, and exports, or a new API route with its handler and validation -- stop typing and start generating. Scaffolding tools let you define a template once and stamp out new instances in seconds.

blocks.description: Plop.js generates files from templates. Run 'npx plop component' and answer the prompts. It creates the component file, the test file, the story file, and updates the barrel export. Every component follows the same pattern automatically.
The Handlebars template that Plop uses. Every generated component gets the same structure, the same prop typing, the same export pattern.
Run the generator. Answer one question. Get three files. Consistent structure, every time.
blocks.code:
npx plop component
# ? Component name: UserProfile
# ✔ src/components/UserProfile/UserProfile.tsx
# ✔ src/components/UserProfile/UserProfile.test.tsx
# ✔ src/components/UserProfile/index.ts

blocks.heading: Speed Anti-Patterns -- When Fast Becomes Expensive
blocks.body: Going fast is good. Going fast in the wrong direction is expensive. There are shortcuts that save you time today and cost you nothing. There are shortcuts that save you time today and cost you ten times as much next month. Know the difference.

blocks.heading: Dangerous Shortcuts
rows: Less typing, no type errors to fix
Bugs in production that types would have caught at build time. Refactoring becomes terrifying.
Use TypeScript from the start. The 10% overhead saves 10x in debugging.
Ship features faster without writing tests
Payment processing breaks silently. User signup fails on edge cases. You find out from angry users.
Test auth, payments, and data mutations. Skip testing UI layout.
One component crash takes down your entire app. Users see a white screen.
Wrap major sections in error boundaries. Five minutes of work prevents total app crashes.
Quick and easy, no env file to manage
Secrets in git history. Leaked API keys. Compromised accounts.
Use .env files from day one. It takes 30 seconds to set up.
You just turned TypeScript into JavaScript. You get zero type safety and the bugs are back.
Use unknown and narrow the type. Or generate types from your API/database schema.
Warnings are not errors, the app still works
Warnings become bugs. Unused variables hide dead code. Missing deps cause stale closures.
Fix warnings as you go. Run eslint --max-warnings=0 in CI.
Everything in one file is easier to find
500-line components are impossible to test, reuse, or understand. Every change risks breaking something.
Extract when a component exceeds 150 lines or handles more than one responsibility.

blocks.title: The One Rule of Cutting Corners
blocks.body: Never cut corners on security. You can refactor ugly code. You can add tests later. You can redesign the UI. You cannot un-leak passwords. You cannot un-breach a database. Security shortcuts are permanent.

blocks.heading: The 80/20 Rule -- Ruthless Scope Cutting
blocks.body: Twenty percent of your features will deliver eighty percent of the value. Find those features and build them first. Everything else is a nice-to-have that you can add after launch. Most nice-to-haves will never actually be needed.

blocks.heading: How to Cut Scope Like a Pro
items: List every feature you want. Now cut half of them. Now cut half again. What is left is your MVP.
Ask: if I launched without this feature, would anyone notice? If no, cut it.
Replace build with buy. Do not build auth, payments, email, file uploads, analytics, or search. Use a service.
Launch with one user role, one payment plan, one language, one theme. Add complexity after you have users.
Use a spreadsheet instead of an admin dashboard. Use email instead of in-app notifications. Use manual processes instead of automation.
If a feature takes more than 2 days, ask if there is a simpler version that takes 2 hours.

blocks.heading: Feature Triage Matrix
rows: Users can create and share posts
Password reset, error pages, mobile layout
Dark mode, i18n, advanced analytics, custom themes
Build when users ask for it

blocks.heading: Rapid Iteration Workflow
blocks.body: Shipping fast is not just about building fast. It is about getting feedback fast, deciding fast, and deploying fast. You need a workflow where an idea goes from your brain to a user's screen in hours, not weeks.

blocks.heading: The Speed Workflow
items: Idea lands -- write it down in one sentence. If you cannot explain it in one sentence, you do not understand it yet.
Branch off main. Name it feature/short-description. No Jira ticket needed for experiments.
Build the smallest possible version. If it takes more than a day, you are building too much.
Push to git. Vercel creates a preview URL automatically. Share it with one person and ask: does this solve the problem?
If yes, merge to main. Auto-deploy. It is live. If no, either iterate or delete the branch. Do not hoard unfinished branches.
Monitor errors with Sentry. Watch analytics. Listen to users. Repeat from step 1.

blocks.description: A dead-simple feature flag system. No third-party service needed for your MVP. When you need A/B testing and user targeting, upgrade to LaunchDarkly or Vercel Feature Flags.
A quick-deploy script for emergencies. When something breaks in production, you do not have time for a full PR review. Fix it, test the critical path, deploy.

blocks.heading: The Complete Rapid Development Cheat Sheet
blocks.body: Here is your entire rapid development playbook condensed into one table. Bookmark this. Every tool, every command, every shortcut you need to go from nothing to deployed.

blocks.heading: Zero to Deployed Cheat Sheet
rows: npx shadcn@latest add button card dialog table
npm install next-auth, create API route, add providers
npx prisma init --datasource-provider sqlite, write schema, npx prisma migrate dev
npm install stripe, create checkout endpoint, handle webhook
npm install uploadthing, define file router, drop in component
Describe what you want, paste the output, review and adjust

blocks.title: The Math of Shipping Fast
blocks.body: Using this stack, you can go from zero to a deployed app with auth, database, payments, and email in under 45 minutes. Not a toy demo -- a real app that real users can sign up for and pay you money. The bottleneck is no longer technology. It is deciding what to build.

references.title: Rapid Development: Taming Wild Software Schedules
